title: Tea on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes; cold + vinegar + 3% peroxide on white polyester only, polyester locks tannin under heat
stainChemistry: Tea on polyester is a tannin stain — black tea harder than green because of more oxidation. Two complications stack: heat sets tannin into polyester permanently AND polyester is industrially dyed with disperse dyes at high heat (the same heat at home drives tea color into the fiber forever), AND alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) DARKEN tannin permanently. Tea with milk on polyester adds the protein-cooked-into-polyester problem (use coffee-cream-polyester protocol). The recurring traps: hot water (sets tannin + locks color into polyester), alkaline first-step (darkens tannin permanently), and hot dryer (yellows polyester over time).
whyThisWorks: Cold water back-flush extracts bulk tannin before it sets. Diluted vinegar disrupts the tannin grip. 3% peroxide on white polyester partially oxidizes residual brown. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap on dyed polyester: hot-dried tea sometimes leaves a brown shadow because polyester locks dye under heat — manage expectations.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): tea is theaflavins + thearubigins (oxidized catechin polymers) + tannin polyphenols. Pro uses cold acid-side tannin spotter + 3% H₂O₂ + acetic acid. Sodium hydrosulfite for set chromophore on white polyester.
defaultAssumption: Tea spill on polyester shirt or athletic-wear, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Black tea is harder than green; tea with milk needs coffee-cream-polyester protocol. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester = pro.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white catch towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the BACK of the spot under cold running water with a white towel underneath the front. 60–90 sec. Cold only — heat sets tannin into polyester permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1:1 white vinegar + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar + cool water on damp cloth, press 5–10 min. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE polyester only. Hidden test on dyed polyester. 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners.
Cool machine wash. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks brown into polyester.

steps: 2. Diluted white vinegar 5–10 min on damp cloth.
3. 3% H₂O₂ for white polyester, hidden test on dyed.
4. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. No heat until cleared.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets tannin into polyester
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets tannin into polyester permanently
NEVER alkaline cleaners — DARKEN tannin permanently
NEVER mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + alkaline
Hidden test on dyed polyester before peroxide

homeSolutions.agent: Cold and Acid First — No Alkaline Panic
homeSolutions.instruction: Tea on polyester is a tannin stain. Two hard rules: NO hot water (sets tannin into polyester permanently) and NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide all darken tannin permanently). The path: cold back-flush → vinegar → 3% peroxide on white polyester → cool wash + AIR-DRY.
Turn the polyester inside out. Hold the BACK of the spot under cold running water with a white towel underneath. 60 to 90 seconds. Cold only — heat sets tannin into polyester forever.
Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Apply with a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes. The mild acid keeps tannin on the lighter side. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE polyester only: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. NEVER hot dryer until cleared. Honest cap on dyed polyester: faint brown shadow may remain regardless.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO alkaline cleaners. NO hot dryer until cleared.
3. Diluted white vinegar 5–10 min on damp cloth.
4. 3% peroxide damp cloth on white polyester only — hidden test on dyed.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. No dryer until cleared.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent on polyester). Stop if alkaline cleaner was used as first step (darkened tannin permanently). Stop on performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 2 home cycles plateau.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets tannin into polyester permanently

neverDo: Never hot water on tea + polyester
Never alkaline cleaners on tannin — darken permanently
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + alkaline
fiberSensitivities: Dyed polyester: hidden test mandatory; honest cap is partial removal on aged
Polyester locks color under heat — strict cool discipline

materialWarnings: No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks tannin
Honest cap on dyed polyester for aged tea

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent on polyester). Alkaline cleaner used as first step. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. 2 home cycles plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tea on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, [black / green / herbal / with milk], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cold back-flush + diluted vinegar + 3% peroxide on white + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no alkaline. [Residual brown / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with polyester experience plus tannin-spotter chemistry

contextQuestions: Black tea, green tea, or herbal?
With milk (adds protein step — use coffee-cream-polyester protocol) or black tea only?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Has alkaline cleaner been used as first step?
Has hot water or hot dryer been used?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black tea is the harder variant. Tea with milk on polyester needs the protein-first order discipline (use coffee-cream-polyester protocol). Most common home failure is reaching for OxiClean alone or baking soda first — alkaline darkens tannin permanently. Polyester adds the heat-lock complication.
